steps.content: A decorator is a function that takes another function and extends its behavior without permanently modifying it. Think of it like wrapping a gift — the original item is still there, but now it has a decorative wrapper that adds something extra. Decorators are a powerful tool for adding logging, timing, access control, and more to existing functions.
Instead of manually reassigning say_hello = my_decorator(say_hello), Python gives you a cleaner shortcut: the @ symbol. Just write @decorator_name right above the function definition.
@my_decorator is exactly equivalent to say_hello = my_decorator(say_hello). The decorator runs when the function is defined, not when it's called. The decorated version replaces the original.

A decorator typically defines an inner function called a wrapper. The wrapper runs code before and after calling the original function. The decorator returns this wrapper, which now takes the place of the original function. When you call the decorated function later, you're actually calling the wrapper.

When a decorator replaces a function with a wrapper, the original function's metadata (name, docstring, etc.) gets lost. The functools.wraps decorator copies that metadata from the original function to the wrapper, so your decorated functions keep their identity.

Decorators are everywhere in real Python code. Common examples include: logging (automatically log every function call), timing (measure execution time), authentication (check user permissions before running), caching (store and reuse results of expensive calls), and input validation (check arguments before the function runs).
Always add @wraps(func) from functools to your wrapper functions in real projects. It's a best practice that prevents subtle bugs when other code inspects your decorated functions.
